commentaries.author: George Leo Haydock
commentaries.content: Hebrew yachmur, which some translate "the fallow-deer. "The Arabs give this name to a beast resembling a hart, which has horns and red hair. (Calmet) It was served up on the table of Solomon, 3 Kings iv. Pliny ( viii. 13,) mentions the bubalus of Africa, which is like a calf. (Menochius) Chamois, (tragelaphum) a beast which has the head of a he-goat, and the carcass of a hart. ) (Pliny, viii. ) Bo chart translates akko after the Arabic, "the wild goat. " Pygarg, another species of goat, (Pliny, viii. 53,) of the colour of ashes. ) Dishon means "ashes "in Hebrew. Goat, (orygem) "a wild goat, (Septuagint; Bo chart;) or ox. "Aristotle allows it only one horn. Juvenal mentions that the Getulians feasted on its flesh; and the Egyptian priests, according to Horus, were allowed to eat it, without any scrupulous examination of the sealers. (Calmet) Camelopardalus. This animal resembles a camel in its head and longish neck, and the panther in the spotted skin. (Pliny, viii. ) Bo chart (iii. 21,) thinks that the Hebrew zamer, means "a wild goat "noted for "leaping.

methodology.step_1: Parse main page with BeautifulSoup for structure
methodology.step_3: Use Firecrawl to get full content from 'Go to Commentary' links
methodology.step_4: Fallback to BeautifulSoup for accordion content if needed
advantages: Records verses even without commentaries for complete tracking
